behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces a route ≤1.5x optimal for 5–20 stops
behaviors.justification: The TSP heuristic is a pure algorithm with deterministic output. Correctness depends only on the algorithm logic and input coordinates—no coordination with other modules, no I/O, no real API calls. A unit test with synthesized coordinate sets (clusters, grids, collinear points) is sufficient to catch failures in distance calculation, route construction, or algorithm bounds.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit test: run the TSP heuristic on coordinate sets of sizes 5, 10, 15, 20 in various geometric patterns (clusters, lines, grids). For each, compute route distance and verify ≤1.5x optimal or known benchmark. Include edge cases (collinear points, single point, two points). This catches logic errors in distance math, route ordering, or algorithm violations of the approximation bound.

behaviors.description: Delivery time window estimates computed correctly from distance and configured road speed
behaviors.justification: Time estimation is a pure formula: time = distance / speed. No I/O, no module coordination, no non-determinism. Failures are simple: wrong formula, wrong units, incorrect config lookup. A unit test with parametrized distance/speed/road-type inputs will catch all real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit test: verify time = distance / speed_for_road_type across cases like (10 mi, 30 mph, highway) → 20 min, (0.5 mi, 15 mph, residential) → 2 min. Test multiple road types (highway, city, residential, etc.) to verify correct speed config is applied. Edge case: 0 distance → 0 time. Catches formula inversions, unit conversion errors, and config-lookup bugs.

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects routes exceeding driver's remaining shift, returning excess time
behaviors.justification: Validation is a pure comparison: reject if total_time > remaining_shift_hours; calculate excess = total_time - remaining_shift_hours. No I/O, no module coordination. Failures are simple: inverted logic, off-by-one errors, wrong excess calculation. Unit test with parametrized time and shift values catches all real bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit test where total_route_time ∈ {10, 60, 120} min and driver_remaining_shift ∈ {30, 120, 180} min. Verify: correct rejection decision (reject iff total > remaining); excess is calculated only when rejected and equals total - remaining. Edge case: total = remaining (boundary). Catches comparison-logic bugs and excess-calculation errors.

behaviors.description: When geocoding fails for an address, service skips it, optimizes remaining addresses, reports which were skipped
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires testing coordination between multiple modules: geocoding (call and error handling), filtering (remove failed addresses), optimization (run on remaining), and reporting (output skipped list). While you can mock the geocoding API, the real failure modes involve data flowing correctly through the pipeline—filtering must remove the right addresses, the optimizer must receive the filtered list, and the skipped list must be threaded to output. These coordination bugs are only caught when modules are wired together.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: input a 5-address list where addresses 2 and 4 fail geocoding (mocked geocoding API returns empty results for those). Verify: (1) optimization runs on addresses {1, 3, 5} only (correct filtering), (2) returned route contains exactly 3 stops, (3) 'skipped_addresses' field lists addresses 2 and 4 with original indices. Use a mocked or test-double geocoding module to inject the failures. This catches filtering bugs, data-threading errors, and reporting omissions across module boundaries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Could theoretically be a Unit test with heavy mocking of geocoding and optimization, but that risks missing real coordination bugs (e.g., filtered list not passed correctly to optimizer). Integration testing with a mock geocoding module ensures modules coordinate correctly.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses consolidated into single stop with combined delivery items
behaviors.justification: Consolidation is pure data transformation: given a list of (address, items) pairs, deduplicate by address and merge items. No I/O, no module coordination, no non-determinism. Failures are simple: missing dedup logic, lost items, wrong merge order. Unit test with parametrized lists catches all bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit test with inputs like [('123 Main', [item_a]), ('456 Oak', [item_b]), ('123 Main', [item_c])]. Verify output is [('123 Main', [item_a, item_c]), ('456 Oak', [item_b])] with correct deduplication and item combination (preserving order or using correct merge strategy). Edge cases: all duplicates, no duplicates, empty items list, null addresses. Catches missing dedup, lost items, and incorrect merge order.
